pages.text: 2.3.2. Low-temperature cracking tests Three-point bending tests were carried out to evaluate low-temperature crack- ing resistance performance of asphalt mixtures. The specimens with 250 mm length, 30 mm width and 35 mm thickness were prepared. The tests were carried on at /C0 10 /C176C with a loading rate of 50 mm/min. Stress–strain curve was obtained during the loading process. Bending strain energy density was calculated to evalu- ate the low-temperature cracking resistance performance of asphalt mixture. The relevant equation was shown as following: dw dv ¼ Z e0 0 rij deij ð1Þ where dw/dv i s the critical bending strain energy density function, rij is the stress fraction, eij is the strain fraction, and e0 is the strain at maximum stress. 2.3.3. Dynamic creep tests The uniaxial repeated-load creep tests were used to study the rutting potential of mixtures. The specimens with 100 mm diameter and 60 mm height were pre- pared. The tests were performed at 60 /C176C. Haversine wave stress pulse was used with a maximum compressive stress at 100 kPa. Loading period include 0.1 s com- press and 0.9 s for rest. Test will be terminated when the accumulative load cycles reached 10,000. The vertical deformation could be measured and the accumulated microstrain also could be calculated by the following equation: e ¼ h=H0 ð2Þ where e is the accumulated microstrain, h is the axial deformation, mm; and H0 is the initial specimen height, mm. 2.3.4. Water sensitivity tests The water sensitivity of the asphalt mixtures containing RFAP and LP was eval- uated by measuring the loss of the indirect tensile strength (ITS) in accordance with AASHTO T-283 test procedure. The samples with air voids of 7% were compacted by applying 50 blows on each sample side using the Marshall compactor. Samples were subjected to one cycle, two cycles and three cycles of freezing and thawing. The result of this test is the indirect tensile strength and tensile strength ratio (TSR). The water sensitivity of mixture can be evaluated using the TSR value as follows. TSR ¼ TS1 =TS2 ð3Þ where TS1 is the average tensile strength of conditioned specimen, MPa; TS2 is the average tensile strength of unconditioned specimen, MPa. 2.3.5. Fatigue tests The four-point bending tests were conducted to evaluate the fatigue life of as- phalt mixture at 20 /C176C in accordance with AASHTO T-321 test procedure. Testing were carried out under the controlled strain mode. The microstrain of 400, 500, 600 and 700 were applied on the specimens by UTM-25, and the fatigue life was deﬁned as the number of cycles, N f, at which the initial stiffness of mixture was re- duced to 50%. Potential of recycled ﬁne aggregates powder as ﬁller in asphalt mixture Meizhu Chen 1, Juntao Lin 1, Shaopeng Wu ⇑ Key Laboratory of Silicate Materials Science and Engineering of Ministry of Education, Wuhan University of Technology, Wuhan 430070, PR China article info Article history: Received 10 October 2010 Received in revised form 20 March 2011 Accepted 11 April 2011 Available online 4 May 2011 Keywords: Asphalt mixture Recycled ﬁne aggregates Performance abstract The main objective of this study is to investigate the potential of using recycled ﬁne aggregates powder (RFAP) as ﬁller in asphalt mixtures. RFAP is a by-product of the production of Recycled Concrete Aggre- gate (RCA). The disposal of RFAP may cause many serious environmental problems. It is necessary to ﬁnd potential usage to avoid these problems. In this study, RFAP is used as ﬁller in asphalt mixture by totally replacing conventional limestone pow- der (LP). Firstly, the properties of RFAP is studied by X-ray diffraction (XRD), Scanning Electron Micros- copy (SEM) and X-ray ﬂuorescence spectroscopy (XRF). Then the indirect tensile strength and modulus tests, three-point bending tests, dynamic creep tests and fatigue tests are conducted to compare the properties between asphalt mixtures with RFAP and traditional asphalt mixture with LP. Results indicate that RFAP can improve the properties of asphalt mixture, such as including water sensitivity and fatigue resistance. However, it may cause a little decrease of the low-temperature performance. All the results indicate that the RFAP can be used in asphalt pavement, especially in hot regions. /C2112011 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction A large amount of construction and demolition (C&D) waste is produced every year in China, while how to dispose them is getting more and more attentions. Normally, the disposal of C&D waste is an environmental as well as resources rationally used concern [1,2]. C&D waste is mainly composed of waste cement concrete and bricks with the former taking the majority [3]. The effective utilization of waste cement concrete is to gain recycled concrete aggregate (RCA), which can be reused in cement or asphalt concrete. Most investigations focus on the coarse RCA. To use RCA in cement concrete and asphalt mixture have been re- viewed and discussed [4–7] . However, the production of RCA is accompanied with a large amount of ﬁne aggregates (<1.18 mm) and powders, which will cause detrimental effects on the fresh and hardened properties of concrete, and thus cannot be reused in concrete cursorily [8,9]. The ﬁne waste is mainly composed of cement mortar and aggregates, these are which remained unused [10] and buried in suburbs usually, consequently worsen environ- mental pollution. However, the reuse of ﬁne waste aggregates has been reported as granular road materials and substitutive aggregates in asphalt mixture. Park [11] reported that both coarse and ﬁne RCA can be used as base and sub-base materials in pavement. Poon et al. [9] investigated the inﬂuence on the self-cementing properties of using ﬁne RCA powder (<0.6 mm) for sub-base. They concluded that the self-cementing property was determined by the unhydrat- ed cement in waste cement mortar, effect of which can be ne- glected on the performance of the overall sub-base materials prepared with RCA. Wong et al. [10] investigated the feasibility of using ﬁne waste concrete aggregates (<3 mm) as partial substi- tution of granite aggre

including indirect tensile tests, three-point bending tests, dynamic creep tests, water sensitivity tests and fatigue tests were con- ducted to evaluate the properties of asphalt mixtures with RFAP. Comparison with the mixtures with conventional limestone pow- der (LP) was also conducted. 2. Materials and experiments 2.1. Materials RFAP was obtained from the grinding of the recycled ﬁne aggregates by grinder for about 15 min, which had the size between 0 and 1.18 mm. Recycled ﬁne aggre- gates were from the waste building cement concrete in Wuhan, Hubei Province, China. The original cement concrete was C30 with the service life of about 15 years. The photos of recycled ﬁne aggregates (<1.18 mm) and RFAP were shown in Figs. 1 and 2, respectively. LP was used as control ﬁller. The basic properties of RFAP and LP were given in Table 1 . X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was used to characterize the crystallographic structure of ﬁllers, as shown in Figs. 3 and 4 . The x-axis is a 2-scale (diffraction an- gles). It can be observed that the main components of RFAP are CaCO 3 (calcite) and SiO2 (quartz), while the main components of LP are CaCO 3 (calcite). This illustrates that RFAP is mainly composed of sand and hydrated cement and LP is mostly com- posed of limestone. The microscopic morphology of RFAP and LP are shown in Figs. 5 and 6 gained from Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM). These SEM imagines show clearly that the surface of RFAP particles is much rougher than that of LP, which may lead to higher absorption. X-ray ﬂuorescence spectroscopy (XRF) was used to determine the elemental composition of these ﬁlers. The chemical compo- sitions of ﬁllers are given in Table 2 . Results indicate that silicon, calcium and alu- minium are the three major elements present in these ﬁllers. LP has higher calcium and lower silicon content, which is consistent with the results of XRD. The 60/70 penetration base asphalt obtained from SK, Crop. was used in mix- tures in this research. The basic properties of the asphalt are stated in Table 3 . The coarse and ﬁne basalt aggregates obtained from Jinmen city, Hubei Prov- ince, China were used in this study. Their major properties are shown in Table 4 . 2.2. Mixture design and preparation Asphalt mixture was prepared by using two types of ﬁllers (RFAP or LP) with the same coarse aggregate gradation and ﬁller content. Aggregates gradation curves for asphalt mixtures were obtained from China highway construction speciﬁcations. Fig. 7 shows the combination gradation curve of aggregates and gradation limits used in this study. Standard Marshall Design procedure was employed to design the asphalt mixture. The optimum asphalt binder content of mixtures with RFAP and LP was 5.1% and 5.0% by weight of aggregates. Both of these two kinds of mix- tures have the ﬁller content of 4.0% by weight of aggregates. 2.3. Test methods 2.3.1. Indirect tensile modulus tests Indirect tensile modulus tests were conducted to understand insightfully the ef- fect of temperature on the mixture in accordance with AASHTO TP-31 test proce- dure. The asphalt specimens with 63.5 mm thickness and 101.6 mm diameter were prepared. Resilient modulus of specimens was tested at the temperatures of 5 /C176C, 25 /C176C and 40 /C176C by the Universal Testing Machine (UTM-25). The test mode was under the assumed Poisson ration of 0.3, 0.35 and 0.4 for the different temper- ature 5 /C176C, 25 /C176C and 40 /C176C, respectively. Fig. 1. Photo of recycl

3. Results and discussion 3.1. Indirect tensile modulus Modulus is the important parameters of asphalt mixtures in pavement deign. Fig. 8 shows the indirect tensile modulus of as- phalt mixture with two types of ﬁllers at 5 /C176C, 25 /C176C and 40 /C176C. As shown in Fig. 8, the mixtures with RFAP exhibit a little higher mod- ulus at 5 /C176C and 40 /C176C, but slightly lower at 25 /C176C. This indicates that using RFAP as ﬁller has no considerable inﬂuence on indirect tensile modulus of asphalt mixture. The modulus of mixtures at 40 /C176C is mainly determined by the asphalt mastics, while at 5 /C176C and 25 /C176C are mainly determined by the skeleton structure of coarse aggregates. Higher indirect tensile modulus of mixtures with RFAP at 40 /C176C may lead to better rutting resistance. 3.2. Low-temperature properties Previous researches [15] pointed that asphalt binder was one of the most important factors that inﬂuenced the low-temperature cracking resistance performance. Therefore, the low-temperature performance can reﬂect the effect of ﬁller on asphalt mixture. As shown in Fig. 9, the maximum bending microstrain of asphalt mix- ture with RFAP and LP is 2112.6 and 2465.1 respectively, both of which can satisfy the requirement value in the relevant speciﬁca- tions of pavement construction of China. The bending strain energy density of asphalt mixture with RFAP is slightly less than that of the control mixtures. The bending strain energy densities, which including both bending strength and bending strain parameter, are more suitable to evaluate the low-temperature cracking resis- tance of asphalt mixture. The results indicate using RFAP as ﬁller in asphalt mixture may cause a small decrease of the low-tempera- ture performance compared with control mixture. Therefore, the mixture with RFAP is more suitable to use in hot region. Theses re- sults require further study as well as other tests. 3.3. High-temperature properties The results of dynamic creep tests, which indicate the rutting potential of mixtures at high temperature, are shown in Fig. 10 . Two types of asphalt mixture with the same gradation coarse aggregates. As seen in Fig. 10 , the mixtures with LP as ﬁller have higher creep strain than that with RFAP, which illustrate that the mixtures with RFAP have higher stiffness modulus at higher tem- perature. Higher stiffness modulus of mixtures with RFAP is con- ﬁrmed by the results of indirect tension modulus at 40 /C176C. It is well known that ﬁller can dramatically affect the high-temperature properties of asphalt mixtures, so using RFAP as ﬁller can improve the high-temperature of mixtures. Fig. 8. Indirect tensile modulus of asphalt mixtures with different ﬁllers. Fig. 9. Bending strain energy density and bending strain of asphalt mixtures with different ﬁllers. Fig. 10. Dynamic creep strain of asphalt mixtures with different ﬁllers. Fig. 11. Indirect tensile strength of asphalt mixtures with different ﬁllers. Fig. 12. TSR value of asphalt mixtures with different ﬁllers. 3912 M. Chen et al. / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 3909–3914

3.4. Water sensitivity Fig. 11 illustrates the indirect tensile strength of asphalt mix- tures before and after freeze–thaw cycle at 25 /C176C. As shown in Fig. 11, the indirect tensile strength of all mixtures decreases with the increase of freeze–thaw cycles. Mixtures with RFAP have high- er indirect tensile strength than control mixtures. Fig. 12 illustrates the TSR value of two different asphalt mixtures after one cycle, two cycles and three cycles of freeze–thaw. As seen in Fig. 12, TSR value of the asphalt mixture with RFAP is higher than that of control mixture. It can be concluded that the mixture with RFAP has better resistance against moisture damage while the mixture with lime- stone ﬁller is more water sensitivity. This is because the speciﬁc gravity of RFAP is lower than that of LP, which indicates the volume of RFAP is higher than that of LP for the same weight percentage of ﬁller in mixtures. And it is well known that the higher volume of ﬁller in appropriate range indicates the higher adsorption to as- phalt binder, which results in better water damage resistance of asphalt mixture. 3.5. Fatigue life Fig. 13 shows the number of cycles to failure at 20 /C176C. The re- sults show that the asphalt mixture with RFAP exhibits higher fa- tigue life at each microstrain than the control mixture with LP. It is obvious that the use RFAP as ﬁller can improve the fatigue per- formance of asphalt mixture at microstrain between 400 and 700. In order to predict the fatigue life of mixture at a much wider microstrain level, the fatigue life of asphalt mixture can be de- picted by the fatigue equation with the following equation: Nf ¼ KðeÞ/C0 n ð4Þ where Nf is the cycle numbers to failure; e is the test strain; K and n are the fatigue regression parameters. As shown in Table 5, the n value of mixtures with RFAP is lower than that of LP, which indicates that the RFAP can improve the fa- tigue life of asphalt mixture at high strain levels. As shown in Fig. 14, four point fatigue tests have shown the existence of a stage during the test characterized by an approximately linear reduction in the stiffness modulus, E, with number of cycles [16]. The rate of damage evolution of asphalt mixture can be calculated by the fol- lowing equation: dD dN ¼/C0 1 E0 dE dN ð5Þ where E0 is the intercept and dE/dN is the slope of the ﬁtted line for the stiffness data. Fig. 15 presents the rate of damage evolution of asphalt mixture with two types of ﬁllers. It can be seen that the mixture with RFAP exhibits lower rate of damage evolution, which clearly indicates that the utilization of RFAP as ﬁller can improve the fatigue resis- tance of mixture. 4. Conclusions The potential effectiveness of recycling waste ﬁne aggregates as RFAP into asphalt mixture is studied through a series of laboratory tests. From the analysis of the laboratory test data, the following conclusions can be drawn: (1) In characterization tests, XRD results show that the RFAP was mainly composed of quartz (SiO 2) and calcite (CaCO 3). The results of SEM reveal that the surface of RFAP particles is much rougher than that of LP. The results of XRF indicate that silicon, calcium and aluminium are the three major ele- ments present in these ﬁllers, and RFAP has higher silicon and lower calcium than LP. (2) The utilization of RFAP as ﬁller can improve the water sensi- tivity, high-temperature properties and fatigue life of asphalt mixture. However, RFAP may cause a small decrease of the low-te

(3) The RFAP used as ﬁller in asphalt mixture is feasible, espe- cially in hot regions. However, further study on ﬁeld perfor- mance should be carried out to support ﬁndings in this study.
